messages.content:
Certainly! Kepler's first law of planetary motion states that the orbit of each planet around the sun is an ellipse with the sun at one of the two foci. 

Kepler's second law, also known as the law of equal areas, states that a line segment joining a planet and the sun sweeps out equal areas in equal times. In other words, a planet moves faster when it is closer to the sun (when it is on the larger arc) and slower when it is farther away from the sun (when it is on the smaller arc).

Kepler's third law relates the orbital periods of two different planets around the sun. The square of the length of a planet's year (the time it takes to orbit the sun once) is directly proportional to the cube of its average distance from the sun. In other words, the farther away a planet is from the sun, the longer its year will be.
